data.title: Amazon forming new group to focus on 'breakthrough' consumer products: report
data.description:
• Amazon (NASDAQ:AMZN) has formed a new group inside the technology giant to focus on “breakthrough” consumer products, CNBC reported.
  • Amazon did not immediately respond to a request for comment from Seeking Alpha.
  • The group, known as ZeroOne, will be led by former Microsoft (MSFT) executive J. Allard, the news outlet added. Allard is credited with having helped create the Xbox gaming console and its Zune music player. According to Allard's LinkedIn page, he became vice president of ZeroOne in September 2024.
  • The ZeroOne team will be based in Seattle (where Amazon is headquartered), San Francisco, and Sunnyvale, Calif., the news outlet added, citing recent job postings. 
  • Amazon has had mixed success with its consumer technology products, which include its Kindle tablets and e-readers, Alexa lineup of speakers and Fire TV streaming products. The company also launched a Fire-branded smartphone in 2014, but poor sales and reception from consumers led to it being discontinued in 2015.
  • In February, Amazon held a splashy event in New York where it showed off a new artificial intelligence-powered version of its Alexa voice assistant.
  • Amazon shares rose 1.1% in midday trading on Thursday.

data.title: Disney adds more subscriber perks to its Disney+ and Hulu services
data.description:
Walt Disney Company (NYSE:DIS) is expanding the perks available for subscribers to its flagship Disney+ streaming service. Subscribers who sign up for the Disney+ program through the Disney+ website will have access to perks such as discounts on Disney (NYSE:DIS) resorts, free items in video games, and a $10 credit at Disney Pinnacle by Dapper Labs, which was noted to sell collectible pins. Subscribers will also be able to enter contests to win a Disney (DIS) cruise and attend movie premieres.

At the end of Q1, Disney+ had approximately 124.6 million subscribers worldwide, which was a slight decrease from the 125.3 million reported at the end of Q4. Disney+ peaked with 153.8 million subscribers at the end of Q3 of 2024. Currently, about 30% of current Disney+ subscribers are on the ad-supported tier

Disney (DIS) also unveiled a perks program for Hulu subscribers. Starting June 2, the program will give subscribers a chance to win tickets to Jimmy Kimmel Live! , Comic-Con and the Lollapalooza music festival. Customers who subscribe to both Disney+ and Hulu through various packages will be able to access the perks programs from both.

"Our fans are some of the most passionate in the world, and perks are our way of thanking them for subscribing to Disney+," stated Disney (DIS) Executive Vice President Samantha Rosenberg about the new features. 

Shares of Disney (DIS) edged 0.2% lower in Thursday afternoon trading.

data.title: Fed Chair Jerome Powell meets with President Trump to discuss economy
data.description:
Federal Reserve Chair Jerome Powell met with President Donald Trump at the president's invitation on Thursday "to discuss economic developments, including for growth, employment, and inflation," the central bank said in a statement.

Powell didn't discuss his expectations for monetary policy, other than to "stress that the path of policy will depend entirely on incoming economic information and what that means for the outlook," the statement said.

He also said that he and his colleagues on the Federal Open Market Committee, will "set monetary policy, as required by law, to support maximum employment and stable prices." Those decisions will be "based solely on careful, objective, and non-political analysis," the Fed said.

After the last FOMC meeting, which took place May 7, Trump called Powell a "fool" for not cutting interest rates. In April, he had said Powell's termination "cannot come fast enough," but later said he had no intention of firing the Fed chair.

Dear readers: We recognize that politics often intersects with the financial news of the day, so we invite you to click here to join the separate political discussion.

data.title: Nutrien plans new Pacific Northwest terminal to increase potash exports
data.description:
Nutrien (NYSE:NTR) is planning to build a major terminal at a Pacific Northwest port and is exploring sites in the U.S. and Canada, CEO Ken Seitz told The Globe and Mail in an interview Wednesday.

The company is searching for a deepwater port with rail infrastructure that can handle bulk potash exports for fast-growing markets in the Indo-Pacific, including China, India and Japan, Seitz said, while refusing to comment on which ports are in the running.

Regulations, taxes and approval timelines will influence Nutrien's (NYSE:NTR) decision about whether to choose the U.S. or Canada, the CEO said, adding that the site should be selected by the end of 2025 and be operational by the end of the decade.

The expansion is part of Nutrien's (NTR) long-term strategy to capture market share in countries with maturing agricultural industries where fertilizer demand is forecast to rise; global demand for potash currently totals 71M-75M metric tons, which the company forecasts to grow to 80M-85M tons by the end of the decade.

However, building major infrastructure in Canada to meet this demand is "very challenging" due to drawn-out regulatory timelines, Seitz said.

Prime Minister Carney's federal government has promised to slash timelines, and potash is included on the list of 34 Canadian critical minerals - good news, Seitz said, that may factor into the company's decision making on the new terminal.

data.title: CME Group, CF Benchmarks to launch crypto reference rates, indices
data.description:
CME Group (NASDAQ:CME) and the crypto benchmark indices provider CF Benchmarks announced on Thursday plans to launch four new cryptocurrency reference rates and real-time indices for Arbitrum, Ondo (ONDO-USD), Near (NEAR-USD), and Sui (SUI-USD).

The reference rates and indices are not tradable futures products.

data.title: Apollo working with JPMorgan, Goldman, others to trade private credit - report
data.description:
Apollo Global Management (NYSE:APO) is teaming up with Wall Street's biggest banks to syndicate investment-grade debt on a broad scale, giving it added liquidity to originate larger loans at a faster pace than it could alone, according to a media report published on Thursday.

The private equity firm is turning to JPMorgan Chase (JPM), Goldman Sachs (GS) and three other banks to act as broker-dealers, Bloomberg News reported on Thursday, citing people familiar with the matter.

In some cases, the banks will buy debt originated by Apollo (NYSE:APO) and keep it on their own balance sheets, and at other times they'll broker and price sales to third parties, the report said. The alliance would also help the firm reach individual clients, who often seek to redeem their investments more frequently than institutional investors, the people said.

While investor interest in private credit has swelled in recent years, trading the asset in secondary markets is rare, and pricing isn't usually disclosed to the public. Apollo (APO) has been exploring building a marketplace that would provide real-time data and intraday prices for credit deals, Bloomberg said. The effort to increase price transparency and trading on secondary markets would blur the line between private and traditional credit. Late last year, Apollo CEO Marc Rowan said that in 18 months, it may be difficult for some investors to distinguish between the two.

In December, Rowan has said he sees levering private assets as "the single biggest opportunity that exists for firms like ours."

Apollo (APO) stock rose 0.2%, JPMorgan Chase (JPM) share were roughly flat, and Goldman Sachs (GS) stock dropped 1.0% in Thursday afternoon trading.

data.title: Organization of Football Prognostics S.A. reports Q1 results
data.description:
• Organization of Football Prognostics S.A. press release (OTCPK:GOFPY): Q1’25 gross gaming revenue stood at €595.0M, higher by 8.2% y-o-y (Q1’24: €549.7M) driven by robust online growth of 20% and strong retail performance
  • Net Profit in Q1’25 stood at €123.4M (Q1’24: €113.3M), higher by 8.9% y-o-y.
  • Well on track to deliver 2025 outlook.

data.title: Kohl's outlines 2025 comparable sales target of down 4% to down 6% as turnaround actions progress
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: Kohl's Corporation (KSS) Q1 2025

Management View

  • Interim CEO Michael Bender introduced himself, emphasizing his retail experience and recent transition: "I'm honored to assume the role of Interim Chief Executive Officer at such an important time for our company... I'm excited to leverage my Kohl's board and past leadership experience to lead and support Kohl's through this CEO transition." He highlighted recent changes and reaffirmed the 2025 plan focused on customer priorities, collaboration, and transparent communication, stating, "Our first quarter performance was ahead of our expectations, and the actions we're taking are making progress with early signs of positive impact."
  • Bender noted the strength of Kohl's foundation with 1,100 stores and over 60 million customers, signaling intent to build on recent momentum.
  • CFO Jill Timm stated, "Our first quarter results came in ahead of our expectations. And while we are in the early stages of our initiative, it is clear that the actions we are taking are beginning to resonate with our customers, and we are starting to build momentum in key areas." She outlined three 2025 focus areas: offering a curated, balanced assortment, reestablishing value and quality leadership, and delivering a frictionless omnichannel shopping experience. Timm detailed the return to jewelry (sales up 10%), a high-teens growth in petites, and continued Sephora expansion, with Sephora net sales up 6% and comparable sales up 1%.
  • Timm discussed an expedited rollout of Impulse Q lines to 613 additional stores by Q3 and the introduction of three new home brands. She highlighted a sequential improvement in proprietary brands—Q1 performance approximately 400 basis points better than Q4.

Outlook

  • Timm reiterated full-year 2025 financial guidance: comparable sales down 4% to down 6%, operating margin of 2.2% to 2.6%, and diluted EPS between $0.10 and $0.60. She stated, "Given what we know today and the current actions we are taking to mitigate tariffs, we believe we can achieve our financial guidance for the year."
  • Management maintained guidance despite Q1 performance ahead of expectations, citing macroeconomic uncertainty and a pressured middle-income consumer. Timm noted, "We just thought it was the right thing to do to hold the guidance despite the beat in Q1, which helps us have the room to navigate through that uncertainty for the rest of the year."

Financial Results

  • Net sales declined 4.1% while comparable sales decreased 3.9%. The difference was due to the closure of 24 stores.
  • Gross margin was 39.9%, a 37 basis point increase year-over-year, attributed to category mix and inventory management.
  • SG&A expenses decreased 5.2% to $1.2 billion, leveraging approximately 32 basis points.
  • Operating cash flow in Q1 was a use of $92 million. Capital expenditures totaled $110 million, with full-year CapEx expected between $400 million and $425 million.
  • Net loss for the quarter was $15 million and earnings per diluted share were (-$0.13), a 46% improvement from last year.
  • Inventory was up 1.7% year-over-year, reflecting strategies to address tariff pressures. Kohl's ended Q1 with $153 million in cash and $545 million outstanding on its revolver.
  • Recently completed a $360 million private offering of 10% senior secured notes due 2030, extending debt maturity to 2029, which Timm said, "provides us with ample liquidity to navigate the macroeconomic uncertainty and invest in strategic initiatives."

Q&A

  • Mark Altschwager, Baird: Asked about strategic priorities and the path to comp growth. Bender replied no major changes to strategy are planned, stating, "we're in the middle of the transformation in early days...it's going to take some time for us to get back to that."
  • Altschwager followed up on guidance and tariff mitigation. Timm explained holding guidance due to uncertainty and detailed tariff mitigation through production diversification, adjusting orders, and supplier negotiations.
  • Unidentified Analyst (for Oliver Chen, TD Securities): Inquired about gross margin drivers and proprietary brand penetration. Timm cited mix benefit from proprietary brands and inventory management, estimating proprietary penetration at around 30% and noting room for improvement as newness flows in.
  • Dana Telsey, Telsey Advisory Group: Asked about store footprint strategy and tariffs. Timm described ongoing annual reviews and focus on 55,000 sq. ft. formats with minimal additional closures expected; Bender and Timm both described tariff management as fluid but manageable within current guidance.
  • Jacquelyn Wang, Evercore ISI: Queried on Sephora rollout and operating environment. Timm confirmed Sephora is now in all stores, with smaller-format shops in smaller locations, and positive Q1 performance, especially in fragrance and hair.
  • Tracy Kogan, Citi: Questioned e-commerce performance and margin impact from tariffs. Timm noted digital sales declined 7.7%, but improvements are expected as brand coupon eligibility expands. She said, "we do think we can mitigate a vast majority of the tariff pressures."
  • Blake Anderson, Jefferies: Asked about customer growth priorities. Timm indicated new customer growth is strong, but core customers are giving less wallet share due to past assortment decisions, which are being reversed to regain their loyalty.
  • Chuck Grom, Gordon Haskett: Queried on operating margin guidance and gross margin phasing. Timm stated the 30–50 basis point gross margin improvement target remains achievable, with greater proprietary brand penetration expected to benefit margin in the back half.
  • Brooke Roach, Goldman Sachs: Asked about women's business recovery timeline. Timm reported momentum building but could not specify when positive growth would return, expecting progressive improvement through the year.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts expressed cautious optimism, focusing on turnaround execution, margin levers, tariff impacts, and customer re-engagement. Questions reflected skepticism about timing and sustainability of improvements, with several probing for specifics on store closures, assortment changes, and credit business health.
  • Management's tone was constructive and measured, acknowledging early progress but repeatedly emphasizing the turnaround remains in its initial stages and will require time. Timm described the environment as "fluid" and underscored, "progress starts with the actions we are taking in 2025 to address opportunities and better serve our customers."
  • Compared to Q4, both analysts and management remain focused on the same key issues, but management sentiment appears slightly more confident due to sequential improvements. Analyst tone is largely unchanged—neutral to slightly cautious, seeking evidence of durable improvement.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The CEO transition from Ashley Buchanan to Michael Bender marks a significant leadership change, though Bender reaffirmed commitment to the 2025 plan.
  • Q1 net sales and comps declined less sharply than Q4, and gross margin improved sequentially. Proprietary brands rebounded, with a 400 basis point better quarter-over-quarter performance.
  • Guidance for 2025 is unchanged (comparable sales down 4% to 6%, operating margin 2.2% to 2.6%, EPS $0.10–$0.60), signaling cautious optimism after a Q1 beat.
  • Analysts continued to focus on core customer re-engagement, sales mix, margin recovery, digital performance, and tariff mitigation, echoing Q4 themes.
  • Management's confidence has modestly improved, supported by early traction in rebalancing assortment and category performance.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited macroeconomic uncertainty, especially pressure on middle- and low-income customers, as a key risk.
  • Tariffs remain a fluid risk, with mitigation strategies including production diversification and supplier negotiations, but leadership noted that "the teams continue to work to reduce our exposure to high tariff countries."
  • Digital sales softness and underperformance of proprietary brands relative to company average were flagged as ongoing challenges.
  • Analyst concerns focused on the pace of core customer recovery, effectiveness of assortment changes, and the impact of tariffs on margin.

Final Takeaway

Kohl's management emphasized that while early progress is visible—particularly in proprietary brands, jewelry, and Sephora—the company remains in the early stages of a multi-quarter turnaround. The 2025 plan is unchanged, with full-year guidance for comparable sales down 4% to 6% and EPS of $0.10 to $0.60, as management balances cost controls and strategic investments against ongoing macroeconomic and consumer headwinds. The leadership transition and operational focus aim to build momentum through the year, but the path to sustainable growth will require continued disciplined execution and adaptability amid an uncertain retail environment.

Read the full Earnings Call Transcript

data.title: SpartanNash reaffirms $9.8B–$10B FY2025 sales guidance with expanded retail initiatives
data.description:
Earnings Call Insights: SpartanNash (SPTN) Q1 2025

Management View

  • CEO Tony Sarsam highlighted a “fantastic first quarter, beating our budget and delivering a record-breaking adjusted EBITDA of nearly $77 million,” and emphasized that “sales increased 3.7% to over $2.9 billion.” Sarsam called out strong Retail segment results, including a 1.6% comparable store sales increase, despite adverse weather that temporarily shut nearly 10% of stores. He noted that “the stores we acquired in 2024 contributed to our strong sales results.”
  • Sarsam discussed the Cost Leadership Program designed to “improve our cost structure and add earnings certainty,” targeting $50 million annual benefits and $20 million in-year gains for 2025. He described several initiatives including leveraging scale for procurement, automation in distribution centers, and new retail labor processes.
  • Sarsam detailed the next phase of the strategic plan, focusing on unlocking retail potential with “new leadership in retail,” operational excellence, and enhancing the shopper experience: “We’re focused on providing the right balance in our retail stores, price and value with a differentiated offering.” He noted the acquisition of Metcalfe’s Market and the launch of a summer bratwurst campaign, which saw “brat sales… up a sizzling 148%.”
  • The CEO outlined three retail growth platforms: expanding capital deployment into remodels, investing in the convenience store sector, and growing the Hispanic food market footprint, with a recent large grand opening in Omaha.
  • Sarsam affirmed, “We beat our internal expectations and we are reaffirming our yearly guidance despite the macro environment.”
  • CFO Jason Monaco stated, “Consolidated net sales in the quarter increased 3.7% to $2.9 billion versus first quarter 2024 sales of $2.8 billion.” Monaco reported “gross profit for the quarter increased to $481 million, or 16.5% of net sales,” and adjusted EBITDA rose 2.6% year over year. He described Wholesale segment “net sales… nearly $2 billion” and Retail segment sales at $947.2 million, a 19.6% increase.

Outlook

  • SpartanNash reaffirmed fiscal 2025 guidance: “We continue to expect net sales to be $9.8 billion to $10 billion, with a midpoint of 3.7% growth. And adjusted EBITDA is expected to be $263 million to $278 million, with a midpoint of 4.6% growth.”
  • Adjusted EPS is expected to be $1.60 to $1.85 per diluted share. CapEx is projected at $150 million to $165 million. The company now expects food-at-home inflation of about 2% for the year, up from 1% previously, as described by CFO Monaco: “Higher food inflation is typically a short-term benefit for wholesalers.”
  • Management stated these outlooks “give us confidence that we will achieve the 2025 targets we laid out in February.”

Financial Results

  • Consolidated net sales increased 3.7% to $2.9 billion compared to the previous year’s first quarter, and gross profit rose to $481 million, or 16.5% of net sales.
  • Net earnings were $2.1 million, or $0.06 per diluted share, compared to $13 million, or $0.37 per share, the prior year. On an adjusted basis, net earnings were $12 million, or $0.35 per share, versus $18.5 million, or $0.53, last year.
  • Adjusted EBITDA was $76.9 million, up from $74.9 million. Retail sales grew to $947.2 million, with comparable store sales up 1.6%. Retail adjusted EBITDA was $15.1 million, down from $17.3 million. Wholesale adjusted EBITDA increased to $61.8 million from $57.6 million.
  • Cash from operating activities was $25.8 million, compared to $36.5 million in Q1 2024. Liquidity at quarter-end was about $270 million. Leverage ratio increased slightly to 2.9 times.

Q&A

  • Chuck Cerankosky, Northcoast Research, asked about Hispanic store expansion beyond Omaha. Sarsam responded the company will “open two or three more stores in the Midwest during the balance of this year” and at least one or two in early next year, with plans for “a combination of converting…fallow or out of business” stores.
  • Alex Slagle, Jefferies, inquired about ethnic store conversions and acquired store performance. Sarsam noted Hispanic stores “have led our store portfolio in both top line and bottom line performance,” with new conversions “up double digits from where it was before the conversion.” Monaco added that remodels generally see “double-digit revenue growth.”
  • Benjamin Wood, BMO, asked about cost leadership cadence and retail profitability headwinds. Monaco explained the $20 million cost leadership benefit is “largely in the back half of the year,” with $20 million in run-rate benefits already in Q1. Sarsam said profitability was impacted by the ice storm and pharmacy headwinds, with a “$3 million” pharmacy impact, and Monaco cited $1–2 million in one-time ice storm costs.
  • Andrew Wolf, CL King, questioned competition, promotional activity, and remodel effectiveness. Sarsam described promotional intensity rising in both retail and wholesale. Monaco said remodels and private label expansion are “a winning proposition,” and noted “double digit growth levels” in categories like gut health and high protein.
  • Peter Saleh, BTIG, requested more detail on food-at-home inflation. Monaco explained the move from 1% to 2% was “largely reflective of what we’re seeing in the marketplace.”
  • Scott Mushkin, R5 Capital, asked about long-term CPG volume decline, SNAP changes, and M&A. Sarsam and Monaco described flexibility to adapt to consumer trends, with Sarsam stating, “We don’t see that as a big threat. We see that as a real challenge and we’re up for it.” Monaco said SNAP trends were “slightly negative,” but not amplified due to a lower share of SNAP shoppers. Sarsam confirmed the company remains “very, very active” in M&A.

Sentiment Analysis

  • Analysts focused on expansion strategy, profitability headwinds, and inflation, with a generally positive but probing tone. Questions were direct and sought clarity on cadence, cost initiatives, and store performance.
  • Management’s tone was confident and solution-focused in prepared remarks, frequently using phrases like “we are confident” and “we continue to execute.” In Q&A, management addressed challenges candidly, acknowledged headwinds, and provided detailed responses, including, “There’s a handful of puts and takes in the overall profit,” and “We see that as a real challenge and we’re up for it.”
  • Compared to the previous quarter, analysts were more focused on the specifics of retail expansion and cost leadership programs, while management’s tone remained consistently optimistic but became more detailed regarding segment-level performance and mitigation of one-time impacts.

Quarter-over-Quarter Comparison

  • The company reaffirmed its 2025 guidance, consistent with the prior quarter, but raised its food inflation expectation to 2% from 1%.
  • Strategic focus continued to shift toward expanding the retail footprint, especially in the Hispanic and convenience segments, with management emphasizing execution and new store performance.
  • Analysts in the previous quarter concentrated on guidance drivers, M&A, and retail comps; this quarter, attention shifted toward the impact and rollout pace of cost leadership and ethnic store expansion.
  • Management’s confidence level remained high, but this quarter included more granularity on challenges (ice storm, pharmacy) and mitigation efforts. Analysts maintained a constructive, slightly more inquisitive tone.

Risks and Concerns

  • Management cited inclement weather (ice storm) as a significant temporary headwind, affecting store comps and profitability.
  • Pharmacy segment profitability remains pressured by PBM dynamics, with a $3 million impact in Q1 and ongoing legislative efforts to address this challenge.
  • Increased promotional activity across segments elevates competitive pressure.
  • SNAP program changes had a slightly negative effect, but the impact was described as not significant due to the company’s customer mix.
  • Management is investing in automation, procurement, and process efficiency to mitigate margin pressures and support growth.

Final Takeaway

SpartanNash’s management reiterated strong confidence in its strategic plan and reaffirmed full-year guidance, citing record Q1 adjusted EBITDA, continued retail segment growth, and successful integration of acquisitions. The company highlighted expanded ethnic and convenience store initiatives, robust margin improvement efforts, and proactive cost management as key drivers for 2025. Management underscored its readiness to address emerging headwinds, including inflation, weather disruptions, and pharmacy pressures, while maintaining active pursuit of M&A and operational excellence to capture market share and maximize shareholder value.
